{{Short description|Film award ceremony in 2008}} {{Use mdy dates|date=February 2025}} {{Infobox film awards | number = 65 | award = Golden Globe Awards | image = | caption = | date = January 14, 2008 |site=[[Beverly Hilton Hotel]]
[[Beverly Hills]], [[Los Angeles]], [[California]]| best_film_drama = ''[[Atonement (2007 film)|Atonement]]'' | best_film_musical_or_comedy = ''[[Sweeney Todd: The Demon Barber of Fleet Street (2007 film)|Sweeney Todd: The Demon Barber of Fleet Street]]'' | best_drama_series = ''[[Mad Men]]'' | best_musical_or_comedy_series = ''[[Extras (TV series)|Extras]]'' | best_miniseries_or_tv_movie = ''[[Longford (film)|Longford]]'' |most_wins=(2) ''Atonement''
''No Country for Old Men''
''Sweeney Todd: The Demon Barber of Fleet Street''
''The Diving Bell and the Butterfly''|most_nominations=(7) ''Atonement''| last = 64th | next = 66th }} The '''65th [[Golden Globe Award]]s''', honoring the best in [[film]] and [[television]] of 2007, were presented by the [[Hollywood Foreign Press Association]] on January 14, 2008. Due to threats of boycotts and picketing of the event due to the then-ongoing [[2007–08 Writers Guild of America strike|Writers Guild of America strike]], the traditional awards ceremony did not take place; instead, the winners were announced during a [[press conference]] at [[The Beverly Hilton]] that was open to all media outlets. The ceremony's usual broadcaster [[NBC]] carried alternate programming hosted by [[Billy Bush]] and [[Nancy O'Dell]] of ''[[Access Hollywood]]'', including an hour-long results special. The nominees were announced on December 13, 2007.{{cite web |url=http://www.goldenglobes.org/news/id/80 |title=Cook, Tarantino, Reynolds and Panettiere to Announce Golden Globe Nominations on December 13 |access-date=December 13, 2007 |date=December 12, 2007 |publisher=goldenglobes.org |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071214102213/http://www.goldenglobes.org/news/id/80 |archive-date=December 14, 2007 |url-status=dead |df=mdy-all }}{{cite web|url=http://www.goldenglobes.org/news/id/81 |title=Hollywood Foreign Press Association 2008 Golden Globe Awards for the Year Ended December 31, 2007 |access-date=December 13, 2007 |date=December 13, 2007 |publisher=goldenglobes.org |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071214020838/http://www.goldenglobes.org/news/id/81 |archive-date=December 14, 2007 |url-status=dead }} The television film [[Longford (film)|''Longford'']] was the most-awarded, with three awards including Best Miniseries or Television Movie. The film [[Atonement (2007 film)|''Atonement'']] entered the Golden Globes with the most nominations, and won the awards for best drama film and original score. It was tied for the most-awarded film with [[The Diving Bell and the Butterfly (film)|''The Diving Bell and the Butterfly'']] (best foreign language film, and best director for [[Julian Schnabel]]), ''[[No Country for Old Men]]'' (best screenplay, and best performance by a supporting actor for [[Javier Bardem]]), and ''[[Sweeney Todd: The Demon Barber of Fleet Street (2007 film)|Sweeney Todd]]'' (winner of best musical or comedy film, and best performance in a musical or comedy film for [[Johnny Depp]]) ''—'' which all received two. == Ceremony cancellation == The Association attempted to reach an interim agreement with the Writers Guild to allow its members to write for the ceremonies. When a compromise fell through, striking writers threatened to [[picketing|picket]] the event; almost all of the celebrities planning to attend, including members of the [[Screen Actors Guild]] who pledged their support for the strike, promised to boycott the ceremony rather than cross the picket lines. On January 8, 2008, the HFPA chose to cancel the ceremony, and replace it with a [[press conference]] at the [[The Beverly Hilton|Beverly Hilton Hotel]], held on January 13, 2008, at 6:00 p.m. [[Pacific Time Zone|PT]].{{cite news|author1=Cieply, Michael |author2=Carr, David |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2008/01/08/arts/television/08cnd-globes.html |title=No Golden Globes Show, Just a News Conference |access-date=January 8, 2008 |date=January 8, 2008 |work=[[The New York Times]] |quote=the Hollywood Foreign Press Association announced Monday that the festive awards dinner will be replaced by a news conference at 9 p.m. Eastern time next Sunday night to announce the actual winners}}{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/entertainment/7176095.stm |title=Golden Globes ceremony scrapped |access-date=January 8, 2008 |date=January 8, 2008 |work=[[BBC News]]| archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20080110235252/http://news.bbc.co.uk/1/hi/entertainment/7176095.stm| archive-date= 10 January 2008 | url-status= live}} [[NBC]] initially announced plans to be the exclusive broadcaster of the press conference, with its coverage hosted by [[Billy Bush]] and [[Nancy O'Dell]] of the NBC-[[Broadcast syndication|syndicated]] entertainment news program ''[[Access Hollywood]]''. However, the network balked after [[Dick Clark Productions]]—who normally produces the ceremony and telecast—reportedly demanded that the network pay an additional "license fee" between $1.5 and $2 million for the privilege. DCP defended the allegations, having stated that it was inappropriate for NBC to hold "an exclusive three-hour broadcast special disguised as a news conference that would bar all other media" without paying the HFPA and DCP a "nominal license fee". There were also reports that NBC came into conflicts with the HFPA over the presentation of the event, centering upon the involvement of ''Access Hollywood''.{{Cite web|url=https://variety.com/2008/tv/news/golden-globes-telecast-up-for-grabs-1117978923/|title=Golden Globes telecast up for grabs|last1=Schneider|first1=Michael|last2=Adalian|first2=Josef|date=2008-01-11|website=Variety|language=en|access-date=2019-03-30}} Due to the conflict, the HFPA took full control over the press conference, and announced that it would not impose any restrictions on who may televise it.{{Cite news|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2008-jan-12-fi-globes12-story.html|title=NBC loses Globes|date=2008-01-12|work=Los Angeles Times|access-date=2019-03-30|language=en-US|issn=0458-3035}} [[E!]] and [[TV Guide Network]]—two cable channels known for their [[red carpet]] coverage during awards season—both carried the press conference, but also reduced the extent of their overall coverage due to the lack of ceremony. TV Guide Network aired a two-hour pre-show and a one-hour post-show, anchored by [[Chris Harrison]] and [[Maria Sansone]] from the network's studio, as opposed to its traditional red carpet coverage hosted by [[Lisa Rinna]] and [[Joey Fatone]].{{Cite web|url=https://variety.com/2008/biz/awards/tv-guide-network-downsizes-but-doesnt-cancel-golden-globes-coverage-24127/|title=TV Guide Network downsizes, but doesn't cancel, Golden Globes coverage|date=2008-01-08|website=Variety|language=en|access-date=2019-03-30}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.mediapost.com/publications/article/73976/no-red-carpet-for-tv-guide-nets-golden-globes.html|title=No Red Carpet For TV Guide Net's 'Golden Globes'|website=Mediapost|language=en|access-date=2019-03-30}} E! did not broadcast ''[[Live from the Red Carpet]]'' at all, and scheduled a [[Marathon (media)|marathon]] of ''[[Keeping Up with the Kardashians]]'' with a break for live coverage of the press conference.{{Cite web|url=https://variety.com/2008/film/awards/networks-set-golden-globe-plans-1117978769/|title=Networks set Golden Globe plans|last1=Littleton|first1=Cynthia|date=2008-01-10|website=Variety|language=en|access-date=2019-03-30|last2=Higgins|first2=Bill}} NBC did not air the official, 32-minute press conference, and instead presented the results over the course of an hour-long [[NBC News]] special hosted by Bush and O'Dell from the ''Access Hollywood'' studio. The results program was preceded by a two-hour ''[[Dateline NBC|Dateline]]'' special hosted by [[Matt Lauer]], ''Going for Gold'', which featured interviews with nominees, and guest predictions from comedian [[Kathy Griffin]], and [[Tiki Barber]], [[Jerome Bettis]], and [[Cris Collinsworth]] of ''[[Football Night in America]]''. The results show was followed by an ''Access Hollywood'' special, where Bush and O'Dell visited the venues of the cancelled after-parties.{{cite news| url=http://theenvelope.latimes.com/awards/globes/env-lauer-nbc-globes9jan09,0,1850272.story | title='Dateline' presents Golden Globes pre-show – The Envelope – Los Angeles Times | work=Los Angeles Times}} {{Dead link|date=September 2010|bot=RjwilmsiBot}}{{cite web|url= http://featuresblogs.chicagotribune.com/entertainment_tv/2008/01/golden-globes.html|title= Golden Globes winners? Not the viewers, that's for sure|access-date= 2008-11-02|author= Ryan, Maureen|date= 2008-01-13|work= The Watcher (All TV. All the time)|publisher= Chicago Tribune|archive-date= 2008-01-16|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20080116071147/http://featuresblogs.chicagotribune.com/entertainment_tv/2008/01/golden-globes.html|url-status= dead}}{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2008/01/14/arts/television/14watc.html|title=Strike Was Unseen Star of the Night|last=Stanley|first=Alessandra|date=2008-01-14|work=The New York Times|access-date=2019-03-30|language=en-US|issn=0362-4331}}{{Cite web|url=https://www.post-gazette.com/ae/2008/01/14/Tuned-In-Cable-bests-NBC-in-Golden-Globes-coverage/stories/200801140253|title=Tuned In: Cable bests NBC in Golden Globes coverage|website=Pittsburgh Post-Gazette|language=en|access-date=2019-03-30}} [[Rob Owen (journalist)|Rob Owen]] of the ''[[Pittsburgh Post-Gazette]]'' felt that NBC's resulting programming was an "over-produced mess", and that one could have learned the results quicker by watching the roughly half-hour press conference on [[CNN]], E!, or TV Guide Network instead (as opposed to NBC's hour-long program with commercial breaks, whose results were increasingly delayed from the actual announcements). He also noted that TV Guide Network's pre-show had a stronger focus on the impact of the WGA strike on the show and the entertainment industry. By contrast, Owen described the aforementioned ''Dateline'' special as "sort of a long, drawn-out '[[Barbara Walters]] Special' without the soft-focus, tears or 'What kind of a tree would you be?' questions", and sarcastically acknowledged its inclusion of analysis from the "noted film critics" of the ''Football Night in America'' panel. ==Winners and nominees== [[File:Daniel Day-Lewis2 Berlinale 2008 (2).jpg|right|thumb|150px|[[Daniel Day-Lewis]], Best Actor in a Motion Picture – Drama winner]] [[File:Julie Christie - 1965.jpg|right|thumb|150px|[[Julie Christie]], Best Actress in a Motion Picture – Drama winner]] [[File:Johnny Depp 2, 2011.jpg|right|thumb|150px|[[Johnny Depp]], Best Actor in a Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy winner]] [[File:Marion Cotillard Cabourg 2017.jpg|right|thumb|150px|[[Marion Cotillard]], Best Actress in a Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy winner]] [[File:Javier Bardem Cannes 2018.jpg|right|thumb|150px|[[Javier Bardem]], Best Supporting Actor winner]] [[File:Cate Blanchett 2011.jpg|right|thumb|150px|[[Cate Blanchett]], Best Supporting Actress winner]] [[File:JonHamm08.jpg|right|thumb|150px|[[Jon Hamm]], Best Actor in a Television Series – Drama winner]] [[File:Glenn Close (11442234855).jpg|right|thumb|150px|[[Glenn Close]], Best Actress in a Television Series – Drama winner]] [[File:David Duchovny by Gage Skidmore.jpg|right|thumb|150px|[[David Duchovny]], Best Actor in a Television Series – Musical or Comedy winner]] [[File:Tina Fey Muppets Most Wanted Premiere (cropped 2).jpg|right|thumb|150px|[[Tina Fey]], Best Actress in a Television Series – Musical or Comedy winner]] [[File:Jim Broadbent 2012.jpg|right|thumb|150px|[[Jim Broadbent]], Best Actor in a Miniseries or Television Film winner]] [[File:QueenLatifah08TIFF.jpg|right|thumb|150px|[[Queen Latifah]], Best Actress in a Miniseries or Television Film winner]] [[File:JeremyPivenFeb09.jpg|right|thumb|150px|[[Jeremy Piven]], Best Supporting Actor in a Series, Miniseries, or Television Film winner]] [[File:Samantha Morton Edinburgh International Film Festival.png|right|thumb|150px|[[Samantha Morton]], Best Supporting Actress in a Series, Miniseries, or Television Film winner]] These are the nominees for the 65th Golden Globe Awards.
